coredata.dc:title: Innovative GQDs and supra-GQDs assemblies for developing high strength and conductive cement composites
coredata.dc:creator: Win, Thwe Thwe
Prasittisopin, Lapyote
Nganglumpoon, Rungkiat
Pinthong, Piriya
Watmanee, Suthasinee
Tolek, Weerachon
Panpranot, Joongjai
coredata.dc:description: Recently, there has been significant interest in using several types of graphene derivatives to enhance the performance of cement composites. There are novel graphene quantum materials called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in ultrafine scales, and none has been explored in cement materials. This work presents the effects of these two synthesized graphene derivatives (Qds and BC) on the flow, bulk density, permeable void, water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, sulphate attack, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and microstructures of cement composites, compared with commercial graphene oxide (GO) and plain mix. The findings show that the composites containing GQDs have excellent fresh, physical, mechanical, and sulphate attack properties. The 1.2% BC composites has the highest compressive and flexural strengths, with 40% and 108% higher than the control, respectively. Moreover, their electrical and thermal conductivity properties increased as a function of GQDs content. The microstructural bridging mechanism regarding the seeding and crystal growth of C-S-H phase were seen. This study offers novel insights into the role of using GQDs in cement composites such that the materials can be used as a high performance, high conductive concrete, which can be a future of the GQDs in the construction industry.

head.ce:title: Innovative GQDs and supra-GQDs assemblies for developing high strength and conductive cement composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, there has been significant interest in using several types of graphene derivatives to enhance the performance of cement composites. There are novel graphene quantum materials called graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in ultrafine scales, and none has been explored in cement materials. This work presents the effects of these two synthesized graphene derivatives (Qds and BC) on the flow, bulk density, permeable void, water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, sulphate attack, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and microstructures of cement composites, compared with commercial graphene oxide (GO) and plain mix. The findings show that the composites containing GQDs have excellent fresh, physical, mechanical, and sulphate attack properties. The 1.2% BC composites has the highest compressive and flexural strengths, with 40% and 108% higher than the control, respectively. Moreover, their electrical and thermal conductivity properties increased as a function of GQDs content. The microstructural bridging mechanism regarding the seeding and crystal growth of C-S-H phase were seen. This study offers novel insights into the role of using GQDs in cement composites such that the materials can be used as a high performance, high conductive concrete, which can be a future of the GQDs in the construction industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Concrete is the most widely used construction materials. However, their production process is very energy-intensive and results in significant CO2 emissions, that pose a threat to our global sustainable development. The CO2 emissions from this production account for 5–7% of global CO2 gas production [1,2]. In order to meet the demands of modern construction engineering and environmental consciousness, there is a pressing need to develop high-strength and durable cement-based functional composites with less cement contents. Therefore, several mineral and chemical additive technologies are developed for enhancing their hydration and performance at both early and later ages.

Portland cement undergoes hydration to form calcium-silicate-hydrate (C-S-H) gel, which contains nanocrystalline regions with atomic structures, similarly to tobermorite and/or jennite [3]. For the investigation in the nano and atomic levels, there is incoming interest in the potential of utilizing nanomaterials and nanotechnology in cement-based materials, leading to the improvement in their macro-properties [4–8]. Various nanomaterials have been studied and the results revealed that the mechanical properties of such cement composites are superior to varying degrees. In an up-to-date material innovation, there has been a wide adoption of novel quantum dots technology to several industries, and these materials include “graphene quantum dots” (GQDs).

Long et al. [9] investigated using GQDs to enhance the dispersity of metakaolin (MK) in cement composites. The resulting GQDs-assisted MK (GMK) exhibits improved characteristics and, when incorporated at 1.0 wt%, significantly increases the compressive strength of mortar by 31.03% after 28 days. In addition, Qu et al. [10] studied carbon dots (CDs) as a novel and cost-effective nanomaterial, synthesized through an improved microwave pyrolysis method. The CDs significantly enhance the compressive strength of cement-based composites by 16.8% at a 0.08 wt% dosage after 28 days, determining their potential as a superior alternative for improving building material properties. Long et. al. [11] examined a scalable and environmentally friendly approach using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to enhance the dispersion of bulk g-C3N4 (CN) in cement composites. The resulting G-CN (i.e., high-dispersion CN was fabricated by GQDs-assisted ultrasonic exfoliation) exhibited superior dispersion, accelerated cement hydration, and significantly improved compressive strength, indicating its potential for advancing the application of 2D nanomaterials in cement-based materials.

Graphene is a two-dimensional carbon-based material with excellent mechanical properties. It has a tensile strength of over 50 GPa and a Young’s modulus of about 1 TPa [12]. Graphene's electron mobility at room temperature is as high as 2.5 ×105 cm2/(V•s) [13], and its thermal conductivity is up to 5300 W/(m•K) [14], greater than the conductivity of carbon nanotube and diamond. GQDs exhibit hydrophilic characteristics. They have a tendency to be water-soluble and show good dispersibility in aqueous solutions. The hydrophilic nature of GQDs is often desirable for applications where interactions with water are important, such as in biological and medical contexts, sensing, or environmental applications [15]. When graphene is incorporated in the cement-based materials, it can be easily dispersed in the cement matrix to form a network structure, which increases the composite’s density of the internal structure. This consequently results in strengthening and toughening the cement composites due to its nanometer size and two-dimensional morphology [14]. Additionally, experimental results found that their thermal and electrical conductivity can be significantly enhanced making them can be used as functional cement-based materials [16].

GQDs are composed of one or more layers of graphene with unique properties with the dimensions of a few nanometers to a few hundred nanometers that combine both graphene and CDs. A number of publications have already been devoted to applications and assembly structure of GQDs via bottom-up approaches since they offer extraordinary possibilities toward the synthesis and modification of GQDs. The synthesized assembly herein is called “suprastructure of GQDs assemblies” (referred to as supra-GQDs). The supra-GQDs is obtained from the post treatment of the synthesized GQDs at 600 °C under N2. High temperature post-synthesis treatment with and without metal catalysts have been employed to transform 0-D CDs into 2-D, 3-D carbon allotropes [17]. The obtained products are mainly composed of sp2-hybridized carbon allotropes such as graphene-like nanosheets, GQDs-nanotubes, turbostratic graphene, and porous carbon framework.

GQDs in this work have morphologies and properties, similar to graphene, such as quantum confinement effects, non-zero band gap, and edge effects. From the review of existing literatures involved, the GQDs have not been previously utilized in cement composites. Furthermore, the GQDs technology in this work is developed from green technology using amine-CO2 adducts as the carbon source (Patent application no. PCT/IB2023/056725). An aqueous solution containing 30% monoethanolamine (MEA) is used to capture CO2 from CO2 emission source. Such GQDs can reduce the amount of CO2 emission when mixed with the cementitious materials, making the resulting composites more sustained. Moreover, the study of GQDs from CO2 gas adsorption is currently lacking. Although some researchers [15,18,19] have recently developed GQDs for CO2 gas adsorption, their potential applications have been little explored.

This study presents novel effects of GQDs and its suprastructure in GQDs assemblies mixed with cement composites on their workability, density, porosity, water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, resistance to sulphate attack, electrical conductivity, thermal conductivity, and scanning electron micrograph (SEM) with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Findings regarding the fresh, mechanical, long-term, and functional properties of cement composites incorporated with GQDs and its suprastructure in GQDs assemblies contribute to a frontier carbon-capture innovation in our building and construction industries with the aims to introduce high performance and high conductive green cement composites.

All the experiments used ordinary portland cement (OPC) Type I conforming to ASTM C150 [20] with a specific gravity of 3.13 and a Blaine surface area of 3765 cm2/g. The chemical composition of OPC was obtained through X-ray fluorescence (XRF) analysis using a Bruker S8 Tiger apparatus. Fine aggregate was well-graded natural sand conforming to ASTM C33 [21] with a specific gravity of 2.56, a fineness modulus of 2.67, and water absorption of 0.65%. The particle size distribution of fine aggregate is shown in Fig. 1. The chemical compositions, specifications, and properties of raw materials used in the experiments are summarized in Table 1 and Table 2, respectively. Table 3 provides information on the impurities and elemental compositions of BC, determined through the Carbon, Hydrogen, Nitrogen, Oxygen, Sulfur (CHNOS) method and Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectrometer (ICP-MS). These analyses were performed using Thermo Flash 2000 CHNS/O analyzers and Thermo Fisher iCAP Qc instruments. Two types of synthesized GQDs evaluated in this study entail 1) quantum dots (referred to as “Qds”) in liquid form and 2) suprastructure of Qds assemblies in fine powder form (referred to as “BC”) were procured from CrystalLyte Company, Thailand. The Qds solution was characterized by TEM, photoluminescent, fluorescent property, and BC powder also characterized by SEM-EDX, TEM, fluorescent property, Raman, and X-ray diffraction as illustrate in Fig. 2. Upon drying the GQDs are mostly self-assembled in the form of the GQDs solid sheets due to hydrogen bonding among clusters, but they are highly soluble in water. The supra-GQDs are insoluble in water by nature but they can regain their GQDs properties by treating with 10% H2O2 as evidenced by the strong blue fluorescent. Graphene oxide (GO) used for comparisons were in a gray and black powdered form. GO was procured from Luoyang Tongrun Nano Technology company, China. The physical and chemical characteristics of the GO and BC are shown as per the specification datasheet and microstructural measurements. Polycarboxylate-base superplasticizer was employed to improve the workability of cement composites. The superplasticizer, which is a light brownish liquid, does not contain chloride and has a pH of 4.97 and a density of 1.060 g/cm3. To prevent the GO and BC for their agglomeration and ensure proper dispersion, the superplasticizer was combined with mixing water. The dosage of the superplasticizer was 1% of the cement's weight, which helped to adjust the composite cement system's effect on the water-to-cement ratio of the fresh cement mortar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis method for Qds and BC
ce:section.ce:para: The Qds were obtained through the electrochemical reduction reaction of a saturated CO2-MEA (monoethanolamine) solution. The reaction was conducted in the W12xD17xH16 cm tank reactor containing approximately 2 liters of CO2-MEA solution. Four pairs of the Ag electrodeposited on Cu foils and Pt foils were employed as cathodes and anodes, respectively. The reaction was carried out under ambient conditions with a cell voltage of −2.6 V for 12 minutes for 10 cycles, with a new cathode replaced at each cycle [Patent No. WO2022185098A1]. After 10 cycles, the solution was subjected to vacuum drying, dialysis, and freeze-drying to obtain the Qds.

The solution obtained from the first cycle, following the synthesis method of Qds, was dried in an oven at about 270°C to eliminate undesired products. Subsequently, the sample was carbonized in the tube furnace at 600°C for 1 hour, with a heating rate 1 °C/min under a nitrogen flow rate of 1000 ml/min. The resulting hybrid carbon (suprastructure of Qds assemblies, BC) was subsequently ground into a powdered form and sieved to a mesh size of 20–45 before being further utilized as an additive for concrete.

The material quantities for ten mixture proportions were prepared per ASTM C109 [22]. The mix proportions contain different dosages of Qds, GO, and BC as cement additives. A control mortar mix was also prepared for comparison (0% Qds, GO, and BC). Qds and BC were first mixed in dry mix at 0%, 0.3%, 0.6%, 0.9%, and 1.2% by weight of OPC. GO was added at 1.2% by the weight of OPC. Each mix was assigned a unique name based on the amounts of Qds, GO, and BC used, such as mix Qds-0.3 which contain Qds 0.3%, and mix GO-1.2, which contains 1.2% GO. These mixes were prepared in the laboratory for experimental purposes. OPC composite pastes with and without Qds, BC, and GO were also prepared to gain a better understanding of their microstructural changes. Fine aggregate was excluded from the pastes, and the other ingredients were prepared similarly to those of mortar mixtures. The preparation procedures were similar to that of mortar without the addition of fine aggregates. The mix proportions of raw materials are illustrated in Table 4.

The initial step involved weighing and batching the constituent materials for each mixture. To achieve proper dispersion of GO/BC particles and prevent agglomeration, the following procedure was implemented: A beaker was used to combine a portion of the mixing water (approximately 500 g) and the superplasticizer. The GO/BC particles were then added to the mixture of water and superplasticizer and manually stirred with a stirring rod until the particles completely dissolved. Afterward, an ultrasonicator operating at 60 Hz (280 W) was utilized to sonicate the suspension for 1 h, resulting in a uniform and homogeneous particle suspension. The mixing and curing procedures for the specimens followed the guidelines outlined in ASTM C305 [23]. It should be noted that for Qds, these dispersion steps were not carried out. OPC and fine aggregate were first accurately measured and added to the mechanical mixer, where they were mixed for approximately 1 min. Subsequently, the remaining mixing water and the particle suspension consisting of water, GO/BC, and superplasticizer were added in sequence and mixed until a homogeneous mixture was achieved. The entire mixing process took around 3 min. Once a homogeneous mortar mix was obtained, the fresh properties of the mortar were assessed. The workability was determined using a flow table test in accordance with the specifications of ASTM C1437 [24]. Following the evaluation of the fresh mortar, it was cast into molds and cured in a controlled environment at a temperature of 23 ± 2 ˚C, along with 95% relative humidity, for 24 h before demolding. After demolding, the samples were promptly transferred to a curing tank filled with clean water to undergo further curing before undergoing testing. Triplicate sample was performed for each test.

The overview of material preparation and experimental studies is depicted in Fig. 3. Table 5 illustrates the experimental details performed in this study. The assessed properties of hardened mortar composites include bulk density, volume of permeable voids (VPV), water absorption, compressive strength, flexural strength, sulphate attack resistance, electrical conductivity, and thermal conductivity. The specimens tested for the 28-day bulk density, 28-day VPV, 28-day water absorption, and 3-, 7-, 28-day compressive strengths were 50-mm mortar cubes. For the 7- and 28-day flexural strength test, beam specimens with dimensions of 40 mm × 40 mm × 160 mm were prepared. Prism mortar specimens with the size of 75 mm× 285 mm were prepared for testing sulphate attack. The hardened prism specimens were immersed in a 50 g/L sodium sulphate solution at 23°C. This test was modified from ASTM C1202, since there is no clear results of the length change detected from test specimens containing Qds and BC. Hence, the change in mass of immersed specimens and their compressive strengths after exposed to concentrated sodium sulphate solution were evaluated. The thermal conductivity test was prepared with dimensions of 25 mm × 3 × 25 mm. The thermal conductivity of 28-day hardened samples was measured by TPS thermal conductivity analyzer (see Fig. 4). The thermal conductivity sensor was placed between two samples. For the electrical conductivity test, the 28-day hardened samples were manually ground in a mill. The tests were carried out 4 times on the cement composite powder using Van de pauw method. The applied voltage was 250 V for all measurements. The average values were reported from testing three samples.

Moreover, the microstructural morphology of the cement paste composites was examined through the SEM-EDS observation by using SUPRA 66VP SEM instrument. The SEM instrument was used to record and analyze the atomic-scale surface morphology of the specimens. To enhance electrical conductivity, the fracture surface of cement composite pastes underwent Au-coating process prior to testing. Subsequently, the coated samples were inserted into the SEM machine, allowing the capture of images throughout a span of 6000 revolutions. It should be noted that the SEM images presented in the study are commonly found morphology during the observation.

The results of the flowability of fresh mortars are presented in Fig. 5 (a-b). The flow values of fresh mortars containing Qds ranged from 99% to 115%. The incorporation of Qds in fresh mortars resulted in improved workability. Higher Qds content resulted in higher flow values, compared to the control sample. As the Qds content increased from 0.3% to 1.2%, the workability of the fresh mortars increased by 10%, 19%, 24%, and 28%, respectively, compared to the control mixture.

Additionally, mixtures with GO and BC had higher flow values than the control mix. The flow of mixtures GO-1.2 increased by 28%. For BC, there was a slight decrease in flow values with increasing contents. This test results agrees with the results reported by Tanvir et al. [28]. However, the flow value of fresh mortar having the highest BC content, 1.2%, was 28% higher than the control. Furthermore, BC presented minimum effect on their flowability of the fresh mortars having higher BC contents up to 1.2%. Herein, all the fresh mixes showed satisfied flowability properties. In particular, the fresh BC mortar (with 0.3%) had the highest flowability (reaching 120%) of all mixes. The enhanced workability observed in all fresh mortars containing GO/BC can be attributed to two factors: nearly hydrophilic characteristics of GO/BC and inclusion of a superplasticizer in the GO/BC solution. The superplasticizer was employed to facilitate efficient dispersion, thereby improving flowability uniformly of the fresh products. The observed greater effect of BC on flowability compared to Qds can be attributed to the differences in the form and processing of the materials. In the case of BC, which is in solid form, a dispersion process was employed using a polycarboxylate ether (PCE) based superplasticizer admixture to achieve effective dispersion within the cementitious matrix. This process aims to ensure uniform distribution and prevent agglomeration, contributing to improved flowability. On the other hand, Qds were in a liquid form, and their inherent properties allowed for easier incorporation without the need for a dispersion process. The absence of a dispersion step for Qds resulted in a higher water-to-binder (w/b) ratio (0.55) compared to BC samples (w/b ratio of 0.42), as the liquid form of Qds facilitated their integration into the mixture without compromising workability. It should be noted that there have been some researchers utilizing superplasticizers to enhance the workability of cement-based composites incorporating GO [29,30].

The 28-day bulk density of the cement composites containing Qds, GO, and BC at different contents is presented in Fig. 5 (c)-(d). Test results revealed that the densities of mortars mixed with 0.3%, 0.6%, 0.9%, and 1.2% of Qds increases of approximately 3.5%, 3.3%, 2.8%, and 2.1%, respectively. Among these, the mixture containing 0.3% Qds exhibited the highest density value, measuring 2172.1 kg/m3. Furthermore, the incorporation of Qds at concentrations exceeding 0.3% led to a relatively smaller reduction in the 28-day bulk density of the mortars.

Furthermore, the incorporation of 1.2% GO in the mortars led to an approximate 4.8% increase in 28-day bulk density. This slight density increase can be attributed to the filler effect of GO and the additional material introduced when GO is used as an additive. In the case of BC, the BC samples exhibited 28-day densities ranging from 2179.2 to 2201.3 kg/m3. The density of these BC samples (BC-0.3, BC-0.6, BC-0.9, and BC-1.2) was 3.9%, 4.0%, 4.4%, and 4.9% higher, respectively, than that of the control mixture. The control mixture displayed one of the lowest density values, while BC-1.2 mixes had the highest density cured for 28 days. Furthermore, the density trend supports the mechanical properties observed in cement composites containing Qds, GO, and BC. It has been reported in terms of graphene nanomaterials, graphene can effectively fill the pores within the cement matrix, thereby increasing its hardened density and densifying its interfacial transition zone. This, in turn, enhances the mechanical strength of the composite [31,32].

The influence of Qds, GO, and BC content on the volume of permeable voids (VPV) in 28-day hardened mortars is illustrated in Fig. 5 (e)-(f). Results indicate that increasing the Qds contents from 0% to 1.2% resulted in a significant reduction in VPV values. Compared to the control mixture, the VPV values of Qds-0.3, Qds-0.6, Qds-0.9, and Qds-1.2 mixes decreased by 42.9%, 40.3%, 36.4%, and 32.5%, respectively. The highest water permeability resistance was observed at 0.3% Qds addition, whereas an increased Qds content of 1.2% led to decreased water permeability resistance. Nonetheless, all Qds-cement composite mixes had higher water permeability values than the control. Notably, the 0.3% Qds addition in the cement composite achieved the maximum reduction in VPV after 28 days of curing, surpassing the control mix. The decline in VPV observed in mortars with Qds can be attributed to the filling effect of Qds. These findings indicate that the incorporation of Qds lead to the enhancement of the pore structure, reduction of overall porosity, and increase in compactness of cement-based materials, ultimately resulting in enhanced mechanical properties.

The findings regarding the effect of GO and BC suggest that when compared to the control mixture, their presence positively influenced the VPV of the composites. For instance, the average VPV value of the GO-1.2 mixture decreased by 51.3%, meaning that better water permeability resistance can be obtained. The formation of more C-S-H gel was assumed when GO was present due to the higher nucleation and growth rate GO facilitated in the cement hydration process [31]. Additionally, the VPV values of BC-0.3, BC-0.6, BC-0.9, and BC-1.2 samples were 46.1%, 48.7%, 49.4%, and 51.3% lower, respectively, compared to the control mix after 28 days. The control mix exhibited one of the highest VPV values, while BC-1.2 displayed the lowest VPV after 28 days. The results from this study align with previous findings reported in the literature [31]. Detailed experimental results indicate that graphene at atomic scales in cement composites exhibit greater resistance to VPV than normal cement composites, suggesting improved durability. The presence of Qds and BC promotes the cement hydration process by filling the pores of hardened cement mortar with continuously formed hydration products. Incorporating graphene derivatives in the OPC structure leads to chemical interactions solely with carboxylic acid without affecting the microstructural C-S-H phase. Therefore, the reduction in VPV value of the hardened mortar can be attributed to the accelerated hydration reaction, which is consistent with the observations from SEM analyses.

The results of the water absorption test performed on hardened cement composites incorporating Qds, GO, and BC are illustrated in Fig. 5 (g)-(h). Results indicated that inclusion of Qds, GO, and BC in the hardened mortars resulted in a reduction in water absorption. The experimental results in Fig. 5 (g) revealed the beneficial effect of Qds in decreasing water absorption, with values for Qds-0.3, Qds-0.6, Qds-0.9, and Qds-1.2 mixes being 37.1%, 30.6%, 25.8%, and 22.6% lower than the control mix, respectively. The lowest water absorption value was observed at 0.3% Qds mix, while an increase in Qds content of 1.2% led to higher water absorption value. Nonetheless, all Qds-cement composite mixes exhibited decreased water absorption values compared to the control mix. In this study, the addition of 0.3% Qds to the cement composite achieved the maximum reduction in water absorption by approximately 37%, when compared to the control mix after 28 days of curing.

Regarding the GO-1.2 mix shown in Fig. 5 (h), there was no significant difference in water absorption value compared with all BC mixtures, but this value decreased by around 44% compared to the control mix after 28 days of curing. This significant decrease can be explained by the fact that GO acts as a barrier and that a smaller critical pore diameter improves the structure of the pores in the cement composite. This is, in turn, lowering water absorption properties. The incorporation of GO in cement composites resulted in significantly lower water absorption values. This is reportedly because GO can make the pores smaller, which improves the hydration reactions and makes more C-S-H gels [32]. As a result, more compact and denser microstructures with lower porosity leading to reduced water absorption value are seen.

In addition, mortar mixes containing BC-1.2 cured for 28 days exhibited the lowest water absorption value. For instance, the water absorption values of BC-0.3, BC-0.6, BC-0.9, and BC-1.2 mixes were approximately 38.7%, 40.3%, 41.9%, and 45.2% lower than the control mix, respectively. The decrease in water absorption is due to enhanced nucleation effect of such graphene derivatives. This effect makes it easier for C-S-H phase to form. Meaningly, there are fewer pores in the microstructure of cement matrix [33]. Consequently, the microstructure of the concrete and their interfacial transition zone become denser, leading to a reduction in porosity and water absorption capacity. Given that capillary pores are the main factor influencing water absorption rate, the results of the water absorption test agree well with the VPV test values. Therefore, mixes with Qds and BC have a lower absorption coefficient can be explained by the increase in small microstructural pores and the decrease in capillary porosity.

As depicted in Fig. 6, a strong correlation (R2 = 0.97) was observed between water absorption and VPV values, contributed to a positive relationship between these two parameters. Therefore, it was anticipated that higher levels of Qds (at 0.3%) and BC (at 1.2%) would result in reduced water absorption, supporting with the VPV data. It is widely recognized that water absorption primarily relies on capillary porosity and surface energy. Previous studies have highlighted the ability of graphene nanomaterials to contribute to the refinement of pore distribution in cementitious materials [32,34].

Fig. 7 (a)-(b) illustrates the compressive strengths of hardened cement composites containing Qds, GO, and BC at different contents cured for 3, 7, and 28 days. Test results revealed that the compressive strengths of all mixes with and without Qds, GO, and BC increased over time, which is primarily attributed to the progressive hydration reactions of cement within the cementitious matrix. For the Qds mix, as shown in Fig. 7 (a), results indicate that mixes including Qds exhibit accelerated strength development over time, resulting in a significant increase in strength. For instance, the 3-, 7-, and 28-day compressive strengths of Qds-0.3 increased by approximately 60.2%, 68.5%, and 20.8% greater than the control mix, respectively. Conversely, as the Qds contents increased up to 1.2%, the compressive strengths of the mortar mixes gradually decreased over time. This is consistent with the findings of Qu et al. [10]. As the concentration of CDs, as investigated by Qu et al. [10], increases, there is a gradual enhancement in compressive strengths across different curing periods. However, when the concentration surpasses a certain threshold, the efficacy of strength improvement diminishes. Nevertheless, the figure displayed that the compressive strengths remained significantly higher than the control mix.

The 28-day compressive strengths of hardened GO-1.2 mix showed a rapid increase, reaching 61.6 MPa, as shown in Fig. 7 (b). Compared to the control mix, the addition of GO to the cement composite resulted in higher compressive strengths at all ages. Previous research conducted by Hong et al. [35] also supported similar findings, attributing to the strength enhancement in GO-modified concrete to improved hydration reactions. The presence of oxygen atoms in the functional groups attached to GO did not directly alter the hydrated crystals but alternatively influenced the hydration process by attracting C3S, C2S, and C3A phases. These phases served as secondary nucleation sites for the crystallization of hydrated products [36,37]. As a result, a higher number of functional oxygen groups had a significant effect on cement’s hydration. A high number of functional oxygen groups bond chemically with the hydrated phase of hardened cement products This led to the formation of a densely packed, interconnected network of crystalline microstructures, consequently resulting a higher compressive strength of hardened mortar.

For the BC mixes, results in Fig. 7 (b) indicated that adding BC in the cement mixes resulted in higher compressive strengths for all contents from 0.3% to 1.2%. The 28-day compressive strengths of the mixtures containing BC at 0.3%, 0.6%, 0.9%, and 1.2% were 15.1%, 21.3%, 24.2%, and 39.2% higher than the control mix, respectively. These results were consistent with the investigation of Long et al. and Qu et al. [9,10]. The compressive strengths of the BC mixed increased as the BC content increased, mainly owing to the strong physical properties of the incorporation of BC. The mix BC-1.2 showed the highest 28-day compressive strength of 62.8 MPa, representing a 39.2% improvement compared to the control. Furthermore, the incorporation of CDs resulted in a notable 39.2% enhancement in the 28-day compressive strength, higher than the observed strength increases reported in the studies conducted by Qu et al. [10] and Li et al. [38].

Besides, comparing the compressive strength at different ages of BC and GO, results indicated that the 3-day compressive strength of BC-1.2 mix was approximately 8% higher than the 3-day compressive strength of GO1.2 mix. However, for later-age compressive strengths, the 7- and 28-day compressive strengths of BC-1.2 mix was not significantly different from the 7- and 28-day compressive strengths of GO1.2 mix. Meaningly, in terms of the compressive strength, the suprastructure of Qds assemblies can accelerate the strength development at early ages (herein at 3 days) when added into cement. Its later-age compressive strengths (herein 7 and 28 days) are similar as the GO. At early-age strength, this addition effect of suprastructures of Qds assemblies results in faster compressive strength gain because they function as smaller fillers and nucleation sites. This will be discussed in Section 3.6 (microstructural evaluation). Li and Shi [39] asserted similar findings in their study on utilizing GO to enhance concrete strengths. Additionally, there are relevant studies [33,37] indicating that the 28-day compressive strength can increase by 30% or even more with the addition of GO. However, GO tends to be relatively expensive, involves a more complex preparation process, and exhibits poorer dispersibility in cement-based materials compared to suprastructure Qds. The abundant functional groups in suprastructure Qds are advantageous for chemical bonding with cement hydrates, and the small nano size of suprastructure Qds facilitates their penetration into the gaps between cement particles. These combined effects contribute to the improved compressive strength of cement-based composites.

Additionally, when Qds, GO, and BC were added to cement, the VPV and water absorption values decreased while their compressive strengths increased. This strength increase can be anticipatedly attributed to the fact that the graphene derivatives can consumed more not only quartz (SiO2), mullite (Al2O3), and C3A from mix ingredients, but also portlandite from hydrating products. This enhanced the hydration reactions, leading to the generation of increased amounts of C-S-H and C-A-S-H gels, consequently resulting in higher strengths. Additionally, the electronegativity of graphene derivatives enabled them to attract cations (such as Ca2+, Na+, and K+) within the matrix. When these cations react with Al, Si, and SO3, they form as C-S-H, C-A-S-H, and ettringite (Aft) phases. The formation of these hydrated phases contributes to the development of a denser microstructure and interfacial transition zone, thereby promoting their strengths.

Fig. 7 (c)-(d) illustrate the flexural strengths of all mixes containing Qds, GO, and BC and cured for 7 and 28 days. Results revealed that the flexural strengths of all mixes increased over time due to a progress of hydration reactions, which were similar to the compressive strength results. The mortar specimens containing Qds, GO, and BC at all contents outperformed the control mixture. These flexural strength results support the conclusions drawn from the compressive strength test in Fig. 7 (a)-(b), For the mixes having 0.3–1.2% Qds, shown in Fig. 7 (c), their flexural strengths are higher compared to the control. Similar findings are found from other researchers [33,35], who observed that graphene-based materials added into concrete mixes exhibited improved flexural strength compared to the conventional mixes. At 7- and 28- day curing ages, the control mixture showed the flexural strengths of 5.3 MPa and 7.2 MPa, respectively. The most increased 7- and 28-day flexural strengths of 8.3 MPa and 10.8 MPa were observed, when the Qds content was 0.3%. The 7- and 28-day flexural strengths of the Qds-0.3 mix was approximately 57% and 50% higher than the control mix, respectively. Whereas, for the Qds-1.2 mix, its flexural strength at 7 and 28 days was roughly 38% and 32% higher than the control mix, respectively. The increases in flexural strengths in cement composites can be attributed to the bridging effects of graphene derivatives which can act as reinforcements like microfibers, as reported in previous research [40,41].

As exhibited in Fig. 7 (d), the GO-1.2 mixes presented increases in 28-day flexural strength, reaching approximately 16Mpa, which was twice as high as that of the control mix. GO played an active role in the cement hydration process, resulting in a denser microstructure in comparison to BC. The abundance of functional groups on GO’s graphene layers facilitated their interactions with the by-products of cement hydration. As a result, a strong bonding was formed between GO and the cement matrix at the interface.

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7 (d), results of the flexural strengths showed gradual increases with higher BC. The 28-day flexural strengths of the BC of 0.3%, 0.6, and 0.9% mixes were approximately 69.4%, 87.5%, and 95.8% higher than the control mix. It is also clearly seen that mix BC-1.2 had the highest flexural strengths. The 7- and 28-day flexural strengths of mix BC-1.2 were about 90.6% and 108.3% higher than the control mix, respectively. It is worth noting that the addition of graphene derivatives had beneficial effects on the bending strength of the cement composites. Like the increase in compressive strength, the incorporation of Qds, GO, and BC also increased the flexural strength.

When compared the mix BC1.2 with the GO1.2, the flexural strengths at 7 and 28 days indicated that there was no significant difference in means between those two groups. Meaningly, regarding the flexural strength, incorporation of suprastructure of Qds assemblies in cement composites has similar effect with the graphene oxide. These sheet-like structures of BC and GO can be used as microstructural reinforcement. They reportedly alleviated to prevent microcrack deflection and hinder crack propagation, thereby enhancing bending resistance and also compressive strength [42,43]. When the graphene derivatives and cement matrix interact, they can form a strong covalent bond between the interfaces. As bending force was applied to the composites, the external forces can transfer from the brittle cement matrix to the bending-resisted BC sheets. Consequently, this leads to notable improvements in the bending properties of the BC composites. Hence, the suprastructure of GQDs assemblies can well perform in both compression- and bending-resisted behaviors.

The permeability of concrete plays a critical role as a protection against external sulphate attack. This sulphate attack in concrete structure can result in concrete expansion, mass loss, and finally strength reduction. This expansion, caused by sulphate attack, is typically linked to the formation of ettringites and gypsum. Test results found that there were no noticeable changes in the length of the prism specimens after sodium sulphate immersion due to the external sulphate attack. The incorporation of GQDs led to notable enhancements in the mechanical properties of the cement mortar, including increased compressive strength and resistance against external acids. This increased strength and resilience enabled the mortar to withstand the damaging effects of sulphate attack without exhibiting significant changes in length. Hence, the change in specimens’ mass and the compressive strength of immerged mortars were assessed as shown in Fig. 7 (e-f).

Fig. 7 (e) shows the results of the sulfate attack on the percent change in mass of the samples submerged under concentrated sodium sulphate solution, including the mixes containing Qds, GO, and BC. Results revealed that the mass of all the mixes increased after 7 days of sulphate up to 1.6%, and seemingly higher than the control mix in most cases. The more content of the BC led to the increase in mass of cement mixes in the greater extent. However, this is not the case for the Qds, where adding more Qds resulted in lowering the mass increase. It is anticipated that these graphene derivatives, due to their large surface area, preferentially adsorb the attacking sulphate ions present in the pore solution, rendering them ineffective in affecting the quality of the AFt products. The formation of AFt products results in the increase of mass, as clearly seen in the figure. The results obtained in this study are consistent with those reported by Sharma and Arora [44], indicating the positive influence of adding graphene derivatives on mitigating the effects of sulphate attack. Moreover, observations from the sulphate-exposed specimens illustrated that no cracks or spalling occurred in any GQDs mortar specimens. Note that the images of exposed specimens were not shown here. The inclusion of suprastructure of Qds assemblies apparently enhanced the pore structure of the cementitious materials, contributing to improved resistance against sulfate attack [45].

The results of the compressive strength test after exposed to concentrated sodium sulphate solution are depicted in Fig. 7 (f). Results indicated that the all sulphate-exposed mixes containing Qds, GO, and BC had higher compressive strength values than the control. After exposed, the compressive strengths of the Qds mixtures (Qds-0.3, Qds-0.6, Qds-0.9, and Qds-1.2) increased by approximate 3.8%, 3.3%, 4.7%, and 12.6%, respectively, compared to a 19.9% increase in compressive strength for the control mix. Specifically, for the Qds-1.2 mix, which incorporated 1.2% Qds, its compressive strength rose from 40.5 MPa to 45.6 MPa after exposed.

Moreover, the test results for the GO and BC mortar mixtures indicated that there was no decrease in compressive strength after sodium sulphate solution exposure. Indeed, the compressive strength continued to rise throughout the testing duration. Following the immersion period, the GO-1.2 mix exhibited a higher percentage increase in compressive strength compared to the BC-1.2 mix. The increases in compressive strength for the GO-1.2, BC-0.3, BC-0.6, BC-0.9, and BC-1.2 mixtures were approximately 16.4%, 19.2%, 11.4%, 10.5%, and 3.5%, respectively, compared to their compressive strength cured in water. The findings indicated that GQDs-cement composites exhibited ongoing hydration in the presence of the sulphate solution during the testing period. The increase in strength observed in these composites can be attributed to continuous hydration and the filling of pores with C-S-H gel. Additionally, the formation of AFt phase within the confined voids, which created the expansion effect due to the increase in volume, could account for the absence of mass loss and the reduction in compressive strength. As the specimens continued to hydrate and AFt formed, the pore continuity was disrupted, leading to a further decrease in permeability due to its tortuous behavior. This reduced pore continuity effectively prevented the penetration of sulphate ions into the concrete, contributing to the improved performance of the mortars containing graphene derivatives against sulphate attack. The enhanced low permeability of such mortar specimens likely played a significant role in their increased resistance to sulphate attack. A study by Jiang et al. [46] advocated that a 12.3% increases in compressive strength for cementitious materials incorporating GO after 135 days of sulphate corrosion. These results are consistent with the research on the sulphate attack resistance of GO cementitious materials, confirming the effectiveness of GQDs and the suprastructure of GQDs assemblies in enhancing the resistance of cementitious materials to sulphate attack.

Incorporating excellent electrical and thermal conductivity into cement-based materials serves important functions such as ice and snow melting structure, electrically conductive pavement, and health monitoring of corroded concrete structures. Test results of the electrical and thermal conductivity properties of the cement composites containing GQDs are shown in Fig. 8. Due to the very low amounts of graphene additives, all the obtained mortar mixes with graphene derivatives entail Qds, GO, and BC exhibited megaohm resistance, which suggests that they were all insulators. Nevertheless, results for the electrical conductivity of the Qds in Fig. 8 (a) indicated that the addition of 0.3% and 1.2% Qds led to the decrease in resistivity or on other words, increase of the electrical conductivity by approximately 59% and 33%, respectively, when compared to the control mix. The incorporation of Qds in cement composites is anticipated to the high electrical conductivity concrete.

In contrast, for the GO mix, results indicated that the 1.2% GO mix had lower electrical conductivity value than the Qds and BC mixes. When compared to the control mix, this 1.2% GO mix resulted in a 29% decrease in the electrical conductivity. It is clearly seen that the addition of GO in cement composites cannot enhance the electrical properties, unlike the Qds and BC.

Particularly interesting is the substantial increase in electrical conductivity when the composite includes 1.2% BC. Additionally, the rise in conductivity with GQDs makes them have high potential to exhibit semiconducting behavior in the electrical properties. This enhanced conductivity opens up a wide range of electrical applications for GQDs-cement composites. The results suggest that electrical infrastructures such as pavement, wall, column, and girder could be fabricated using the GQDs-cement composites. According to the percolation theory, the resistivity of composite media does not change linearly with the content of conductive particles. Instead, there exists a critical point, known as the percolation threshold, where a significant and sudden change in resistivity occurs, leading to resistivity stabilization. In this situation, the cement mortar specimen can be analyzed using the parallel and series model of composite theory, observing it as a resistance [47,48].

The discussed test results have exhibited the significant improvement in the incorporation of graphene derivatives (i.e. Qds, GO, and BC) on the compressive strength and flexural strength of hardened cement composites. This section also discussed experimental results of the thermal conductivity of cement composites containing Qds, GO, and BC, as shown in Fig. 8 (b). The thermal conductivity measurements of these graphene derivatives indicated a trend of enhancement for all mixtures. When the Qds content was 0.3% and 1.2%, the conductivity of sample had no significant difference. This is also the case for GO-1.2. This change in thermal conductivity properties can be explained by the fact that graphene in nature has a high thermal conductivity [49] and is spread out evenly in the cement matrix. The presence of graphene particles establishes a favorable thermal conductive network, consequently reinforcing heat transfer throughout the matrix of cement. Additionally, more microstructural pores mean more air volumes in the structure, meaning that the lower thermal conductivity is seen due to air cavities. The most significant increase in the thermal conductivity was achieved with BC-0.3 mix, resulting in an approximate 64% increase compared to the control mix. This outcome can be attributed to the porosity of the filtered BC sample. However, when increasing the BC contents up to 1.2%, the thermal conductivity reduced.

Cui et al. [50] investigated the thermal conductivity of graphene-reinforced cement mortar. Their findings also found the improvement of thermal conductivity, conformed to the results of this study. This addition of graphene derivatives to the cement paste leads to a significant enhancement in the thermal diffusivity of the composite. As a result, the temperature gradient effect (30–90˚C) caused by cement hydration in large-scale concrete structures can be mitigated, thereby reducing the risk of thermal cracking and enhancing the thermal integrity and durability of concrete structures [48]. Notably, the thermal conductivity was observed to rise with an increase in the GQDs content within the composite. This enhanced thermal conductivity in the hydrated GQDs cement composite highlights the heat sink capacity of graphene. This is beneficial particularly in the special residences and infrastructures in cold regions, where solar heat can warm up the construction during the daytime, resulting in lowering heating energy [40].

The summary of the effects of addition of Qds, GO, and BC in cement composites on their properties is given in Table 6. The summary indicated that all the cement composites containing different graphene derivatives had significantly improved performance on their flowability, density, VPV, water absorption, compressive strength, and flexural strength. All graphene derivatives-added cement composites result in good fresh and machinal properties, For the durability (i.e. sulphate attack resistance), the GO and BC composites show the excellent performance, while the Qds shows somewhat improved performance. For the cement composites used as functional materials, results concluded that the BC composites show excellent performance, whereas the Qds shows significant/somewhat improvement. In contrast, the GO composites with this level of loadings likely neither a good electrical conductivity nor thermal conductivity materials. The results concluded that the suprastructure of GQDs assemblies can be a novel material for future high-performance and conductive composites.

The enhancements in mechanical and durability properties of the GQDs cement composite can be attributed to its microstructure. Testing with SEM-EDS was carried out on hardened Qds, GO, and BC cement composites samples to observe their microstructural properties. Fig. 9 illustrate the SEM micrographs of hardened cement composites cured for 28 days containing 1.2% BC at different magnification levels. The micrographs indicated that the hydrating products were formed. There is clearly seen the precipitation of C-S-H gel and CH. In the micrographs with 6000X and 15,000X magnification levels, there is the bridging of the needle-like C-S-H phase. As aforementioned, the graphene derivatives act as seeding of nucleation of C-S-H phase, accelerating the crystal growth rate. This is anticipated to the compacted microstructure and narrowing the interfacial transition zone, leading to enhanced mechanical properties and long-term durability. Moreover, no micro-crack was observed in the micrographs. It should be noted that the Qds was also observed, but the SEM micrographs exhibited a similar observation as the BC. In the figure, the EDS spectrum of various cement composites was performed. Results indicate that the chemical compositions of OPC, Qds, GO, and BC mixes were not significantly different. This is because only small portions of the additives were added.

The overall mechanisms and the effects of using graphene derivatives on the properties of cement composites are shown in Fig. 10. A novel GQDs and the infrastructure in their assembly was found beneficial to the cement composites by enhancing fresh, physical, mechanical, sulphate attack properties. These composites also enhance their electrical and thermal conductivity. The results present that such composites can be used as high strength, high thermal and electrical conductivity construction materials, which extensively broaden the structural and functional applications of concrete.

This study investigates various aspects of the cement composites, incorporating three different graphene nanomaterials (Qds, GO, and BC) at concentrations ranging from 0.3–1.2% by cement weight. The aspects examined include workability, physical and mechanical properties, durability, electrical and thermal conductivity, and microstructure. The key findings were outlined below:
1)
The workability of the composites gradually increased with higher Qds content due to their hydrophilic nature. In the case of GO and BC mixes, the workability also improved due to their nearly hydrophobic characteristics and the small addition of superplasticizer in the GO and BC solutions.
2)
The physical properties of the Qds, GO, and BC composites demonstrated promising enhancements compared to the control.
3)
The addition of Qds, GO, and BC resulted in enhanced compressive and flexural strengths when compared to the control samples. The 0.3% Qds composite exhibited optimum performance. The mechanical properties improved due to GO's excellent dispersibility in water and the significant participation of functional groups in forming chemical bonds with cement hydration products. This effectively densified and bridged the composite matrix at the nano-micro level. Moreover, an increasing trend in mechanical properties was observed with higher proportions of BC in the composite. BC composites showed improved mechanical properties because of BC's high physical strength and the presence of a lower content of functional groups, which formed bonds with cement hydration products.
4)
The Qds, GO, and BC mixtures demonstrated excellent resistance to expansion strains when exposed to external sulphate attack. Throughout the entire testing period, no cracks were observed in the prism specimens. Moreover, there was no evidence of mass loss or a reduction in compressive strength in any of the mixes subjected to the sulphate attack. These results point to ongoing hydration of the specimens, the formation of Aft within the confined voids, and the absence of gypsum formation. Their excellent performance in sulphate environment can be attributed to their low permeability and the overall high quality of the specimens.
5)
The GQDs composites, unlike the GO, exhibited increased electrical and thermal conductivity. These findings suggested that incorporating GQDs enhanced the thermal properties of the hydrated cement, indicating potential for reducing early-age thermal cracking and improving the overall durability of concrete structures. This is the main advantage of using GQDs composites in special applications.
6)
For SEM microstructural analysis, adding Qds, GO, or BC to the cement paste makes the C-S-H phase denser, and the bridging mechanisms of C-S-H was apparently seen.

Ongoing research is to develop large-scale concrete specimens which will be tested for as thermal conductive concrete and brick for specific applications such as a thermal conductive pavement and paving block.
